title: encodeRgba8Pyramid and downscaleRgba16 imported from src/index but do not exist in facade.ts
description: pyramid-bridge-runtime.test.ts imports `encodeRgba8Pyramid` and `downscaleRgba16` from `../src/index`, which re-exports only `facade.ts` and `loader.ts`. Neither function exists anywhere in the current facade.ts. The runtime tests will fail at import time with 'not a function' or undefined. pyramid-bridge.test.ts additionally asserts that facade.ts source contains `export async function encodeRgba8Pyramid` (line 21) and `export async function downscaleRgba16` (line 36), which will also fail. The WASM bridge is missing `jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_with_sidecars_v2` and `jxl_wasm_downscale_rgba16` in bridge.cpp per the same test, and exports.txt already lists them as expected symbols, creating a three-way contract gap between exports.txt, bridge.cpp, and facade.ts.

title: exports.txt lists many new WASM symbols that have no corresponding TypeScript declarations in facade.ts or LibjxlWasmModule interface
description: exports.txt contains ~25 symbols that do not appear in the `LibjxlWasmModule` interface in facade.ts: `_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_x`, `_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_with_sidecars_v2`, `_jxl_wasm_downscale_rgba16`, `_jxl_wasm_transcode_jpeg_to_jxl_v2`, `_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_with_metadata_ec`, `_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_with_metadata_v2`, `_jxl_wasm_enc_create_image_z`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_frame_index`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_frame_duration`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_frame_name_ptr`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_is_last_frame`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_anim_ticks_per_second`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_anim_loop_count`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_seek_to_frame`, `_jxl_wasm_encode_with_gain_map`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_has_gain_map`, `_jxl_wasm_dec_take_gain_map`, `_jxl_wasm_enc_set_metadata`, and others. These are declared in the WASM binary but TypeScript treats them as `any`-casted via `(module as any)._...` or simply unreachable, bypassing all type safety. New callers cannot discover these capabilities and getCapabilities() does not probe them.
code_excerpt:
// exports.txt lines 33-63 (sample):
_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_x
_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_with_sidecars_v2
_jxl_wasm_downscale_rgba16
_jxl_wasm_transcode_jpeg_to_jxl_v2
_jxl_wasm_dec_frame_index
_jxl_wasm_dec_seek_to_frame
_jxl_wasm_encode_with_gain_map
// None of these appear in LibjxlWasmModule interface in facade.ts

title: compare() in ButteraugliComparator returns number but is synchronous while the public JxlDecoder/JxlEncoder interfaces are async — mixing sync and async contracts at the same abstraction level
description: ButteraugliComparator.compare() is declared synchronous (returns `number`), while ButteraugliComparator.create() is async and computeButteraugli() is async. The synchronous compare() internally calls `mallocOrThrow`, writes to WASM heap, and calls a WASM function — all of which can throw from the same heap-corruption or OOM paths that the async counterparts guard with try/finally. If the WASM module is ever replaced (e.g. via setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting) between `create()` and `compare()` the cached module reference in the comparator will diverge silently since the comparator stores its own `module` reference from construction. This is a contract inconsistency callers building wrappers around the module lifecycle cannot guard against.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: encodeTiledRgba8 validates tileSize >= 16 but encodeTileContainer (called by both rgba8/rgba16 paths) validates tileSize >= 1, creating inconsistent contract for rgba16 variant
description: encodeTiledRgba8 (line 889) enforces `tileSize < 16` as the minimum. encodeTileContainerRgba8/Rgba16 both delegate to encodeTileContainer (line 1082) which only enforces `tileSize < 1`. So calling encodeTileContainerRgba8 with tileSize=2 succeeds, but calling encodeTiledRgba8 with tileSize=2 throws. Two public APIs that do conceptually the same thing (tiled JXL encoding) have different minimum tileSize contracts. The WASM bridge imposes its own limits; the facade enforcement is inconsistently documented.

title: serializeExtraChannelsForWasm writes plane_ptr/plane_size fields at offsets 12 and 16, but the returned DataView is used by callers to overwrite those same fields — if callers use the original buffer before re-copying to WASM heap the descriptor is double-written and stale
description: serializeExtraChannelsForWasm returns `{ buffer, view }` where `view` is a DataView over `buffer`. The contract states 'plane_ptr/plane_size left 0 by serialize; filled by caller after malloc'. In facade.test.ts (lines 1418-1425) the caller writes into `descDv` then copies the entire `descBuf` to WASM at `descPtr`. The copy at line 1413 happens before the plane pointers are written (lines 1418-1425), and a second copy re-syncs at line 1428. This two-copy pattern is undocumented in the function signature — the `view` points into the JS-side ArrayBuffer not the WASM heap, so any write to `descDv` after the first copy is not visible in WASM until re-copied. The contract doc says 'filled by caller after malloc' but does not specify that a second HEAPU8.set is needed, creating a subtle serialization correctness trap.

title: EXTRA_TYPE_TO_JXL maps 'selection' to JXL value 3 but JXL spec defines JXL_CHANNEL_SELECTION as 3 while the comment layout lists 'spot'=2 and 'selection'=3 — undocumented, and ExtraChannelType 'unknown' silently maps to 15 with no warning
description: The EXTRA_TYPE_TO_JXL lookup falls back with `?? 15` for any unrecognized type — including future ExtraChannelType values added to the TypeScript union without updating the map. If a new type is added to the `ExtraChannelType` union in one PR and the map isn't updated, encoding silently uses type 15 ('unknown') instead of failing, making the contract break invisible at runtime. The public ExtraChannel type is extensible (it includes 'reserved0'...'reserved7'), so this silent-fallback is an invariant that callers cannot observe.

title: LibjxlEncoder.finish() is silently idempotent — calling it twice resolves the finishResolve promise and then leaves finishResolve null on subsequent calls, but the JxlEncoder interface does not document whether finish() is idempotent
description: The `JxlEncoder` public interface declares `finish(): void | Promise<void>`. The implementation sets `this.finished = true` and calls `this.finishResolve?.()`, which is safe on repeated calls (the optional chain is a no-op after the first). However, `chunks()` also sets `this.chunksStarted` and throws on second call with 'may only be consumed once'. The asymmetry — finish() is idempotent but chunks() is not — is unspecified in the interface contract. Callers that call finish() twice (e.g. in error cleanup paths) will not observe an error, which can mask double-finish bugs.

title: DecodeEvent 'budget_exceeded' is missing optional region and sourceScale fields present in 'progress' and 'final' events, creating inconsistent shape for consumers that handle all pixel-bearing events uniformly
description: The 'progress' and 'final' event variants both carry `region?`, `sourceScale?`, `progressiveRegion?`, `regionFallback?`, `progressiveSequence?`, and `passOrdinal?`. The 'budget_exceeded' variant only carries `stage`, `info`, `pixels`, `format`, and `pixelStride`. Consumers that dispatch on all pixel-carrying event types (progress | final | budget_exceeded) must special-case budget_exceeded to avoid missing-field crashes. The decode-handler contract says 'budget_exceeded ends the frame stream gracefully', so consumers that aggregate pixel frames across event types need knowledge of which events carry which fields. No comment or JSDoc clarifies the intentional omission on budget_exceeded.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: computeButteraugli does not validate that width and height together describe a valid RGBA8 image (overflow guard only, no zero-dimension check when pixelSize happens to be > 0)
description: butteraugliPixelSize (called by computeButteraugli and ButteraugliComparator.create) throws when `pixelSize <= 0` or when `view.byteLength < pixelSize`. However, a width or height of 0 produces pixelSize=0 and the guard `pixelSize <= 0` catches it. But a width of 0 with height of 1 gives pixelSize=0 and would also pass `!Number.isInteger(width)` check fine. The more subtle gap: computeButteraugli validates `width > 0 && height > 0` at butteraugliPixelSize, but computePsnrWasm and computeSsimWasm (lines 787-839) do NOT call butteraugliPixelSize and instead compute `pixelSize = width * height * 4` without any dimension validation, silently passing 0 to _malloc when width=0.

title: OOM on metadata malloc silently drops all three metadata boxes (ICC + EXIF + XMP) together even when only one allocation fails, losing potentially valid metadata
description: When any one of the three metadata mallocs fails (iccPtr, exifPtr, or xmpPtr), the guard at line 2225 frees all and zeros all sizes, proceeding with a pixels-only encode. This means a 4-byte EXIF OOM also silently drops a successfully allocated 16 KB ICC profile. The contract documented in JSDoc (`preserveIcc`, `preserveMetadata`) says metadata is preserved; this silently violates it on partial OOM with only a console.warn. Callers that need ICC fidelity cannot distinguish this degraded path from a normal pixels-only encode.

category: no contract testing between TypeScript and WASM FFI
title: No runtime contract test verifies that the LibjxlWasmModule interface field layout matches the actual WASM binary exports at load time
description: The LibjxlWasmModule interface (lines 330-410) declares ~60 optional WASM function symbols. Currently, getCapabilities() probes about 10 of them. There is no test or runtime check that validates the actual WASM binary's exported symbol set against the interface (beyond the build-time exports.txt validation in build.mjs). A WASM rebuild that drops or renames an optional symbol (e.g. `_jxl_wasm_buffer_error` becomes non-optional) would silently fall back to 0 error codes with no diagnostic. An opportunity exists to add a post-load contract assertion function (e.g. `assertWasmContractVersion()`) that cross-checks the exported symbol names against the declared interface at startup.

title: perceptualConstancyApplyBulk falls back to identity (no-op copy) when the WASM function is absent but returns Promise<void> without signalling that no processing occurred — callers cannot distinguish processed from passthrough
description: When neither `_perceptual_apply_full_avx2` nor `_perceptual_apply_full` are present in the WASM binary, the function silently copies input to output (lines 3172-3174) with no return value, error, or metric emission to indicate the passthrough. The scalar branch is also documented as 'no-op warn; users should rebuild' but simply copies without any actual perceptual transform applied. Callers that depend on perceptual correction will silently receive unprocessed data. The contract says 'fastest available C++ path' but the fallback contract — 'I did nothing' — is invisible.
